lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: So far we have built truths. But what about falsehoods? What about statements that **cannot** be true?

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Negation. $\\lnot P$ — read "not $P$." But what does it mean to prove a negative?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: You assume $P$ and derive a contradiction — the impossible constant $\\bot$ (bottom). `(not-intro F N M)` says: from assuming $F$, step $N$ proves something and step $M$ proves its negation. That is absurd. Hence $\\lnot F$.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: And once you have a contradiction — $\\bot$ — you have everything. `(ex-falso N F)` — [ex-falso-quodlibet|from falsehood, anything follows]. Give it a contradiction at step $N$ and any formula $F$, and it hands you $F$.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: There is also double-negation elimination. `(dne N)` — if $\\lnot \\lnot P$, then $P$. Two wrongs do not make a right, but two nots make a yes.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Negation is the guard at the gate. It marks the boundary between the possible and the impossible.
